profile.summary: Grand Canyon National Park is a national park of the United States located in northwestern Arizona, the 15th site to have been named as a national park. The park's central feature is the Grand Canyon, a gorge of the Colorado River, which is often considered one of the Wonders of the World. The park, which covers 1,217,262 acres (1,901.972 sq mi; 4,926.08 km2) of unincorporated area in Coconino and Mohave counties, received more than 4.9 million recreational visitors in 2024.
profile.story: Here is the story of Grand Canyon National Park. Grand Canyon National Park is a national park of the United States located in northwestern Arizona, the 15th site to have been named as a national park. The park's central feature is the Grand Canyon, a gorge of the Colorado River, which is often considered one of the Wonders of the World. The park, which covers 1,217,262 acres (1,901.972 sq mi; 4,926.08 km2) of unincorporated area in Coconino and Mohave counties, received more than 4.9 million recreational visitors in 2024. The Grand Canyon was designated a World Heritage Site by UNESCO in 1979. The park celebrated its 100th anniversary on February 26, 2019. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

things_to_see.image_caption: Arizona Steakhouse in the village, is one of several dining options within Bright Angel Lodge.

visitor_centers.title: Grand Canyon Visitor Center (South Rim)
visitor_centers.description: Trip planning and hiking information is available through exhibit kiosks and sidewalk signs outside of the building.

visitor_centers.description: In the Village Historic District, near Hopi House and El Tovar Hotel, Verkamp's Visitor Center features exhibits that focus on the Grand Canyon Community; what it was like to live and work here on the brink of one of the seven natural wonders.

campgrounds.title: Desert View Campground (Reservations Required)
campgrounds.description: At the East Entrance, Desert View Campground offers a peaceful setting with 49 campsites.
campgrounds.image_caption: Maximum vehicle length is 30 feet.

campgrounds.title: Mather Campground - South Rim
campgrounds.description: Mather Campground is located in Grand Canyon Village on the South Rim of Grand Canyon National Park.
campgrounds.image_caption: Checking in at the Mather Campground Registration Kiosk

campgrounds.title: Trailer Village RV Park - South Rim
campgrounds.description: Trailer Village is the only in-park RV campground with full hookups (sewage, water, and electrical with 30 amp and 50 amp sites available) Open year-round, Trailer Village features paved pull-through sites which can accommodate vehicles up to 50 feet long.
campgrounds.image_caption: Full hook-ups are available at Trailer Village on the South Rim.

source_pack.operating_hours: Both Desert View (East Entrance) and South Entrance are Open 24 hours/day and 7 Days/week. Credit cards, and America the Beautiful passes are accepted for entry. Cash is not accepted at this time. The South Entrance Station, near Tusayan, Arizona, receives the most traffic, an experiences long lines and up to 2 hour wait times between 9:30 am and 4 pm. The East Entrance at Desert View - 32 miles east of the village (accessed via US 89 to Cameron, AZ, then west on SR 64, usually has shorter wait times.

source_pack.source_note: Official NPS source pack with live park details captured at catalog build time.
source_pack.extract: Entirely within the state of Arizona, the park encompasses 278 miles (447 km) of the Colorado River and adjacent uplands. Located on the ancestral homelands of 11 present day Tribal Communities, Grand Canyon is one of the most spectacular examples of erosion anywhere in the world—a mile deep canyon unmatched in the incomparable vistas it offers visitors from both north and south rims.

profile.story: Here is the story of Yellowstone National Park. Yellowstone National Park is a national park of the United States located in the northwest corner of the state of Wyoming, with small portions extending into Montana and Idaho. It was established by the 42nd U.S. Congress through the Yellowstone National Park Protection Act and signed into law by President Ulysses S. Grant on March 1, 1872. Yellowstone was the first national park in the US, and is also widely understood to be the first national park in the world. The park is known for its wildlife and its many geothermal features, especially the Old Faithful geyser, one of its most popular. While it represents many types of biomes, subalpine forest is the most abundant. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.wiki_extract:
Yellowstone National Park is a national park of the United States located in the northwest corner of the state of Wyoming, with small portions extending into Montana and Idaho. It was established by the 42nd U.S. Congress through the Yellowstone National Park Protection Act and signed into law by President Ulysses S. Grant on March 1, 1872. Yellowstone was the first national park in the US, and is also widely understood to be the first national park in the world. The park is known for its wildlife and its many geothermal features, especially the Old Faithful geyser, one of its most popular. While it represents many types of biomes, subalpine forest is the most abundant. It is part of the South Central Rockies forests ecoregion.
While Native Americans have lived in the Yellowstone region for at least 11,000 years, aside from visits by mountain men during the early-to-mid-19th century, organized exploration did not begin until the late 1860s. Management and control of the park originally fell under the jurisdiction of the U.S. Department of the Interior, the first secretary of the interior to supervise the park being Columbus Delano. However, the U.S. Army was eventually commissioned to oversee the management of Yellowstone for 30 years between 1886 and 1916. In 1917, the administration of the park was transferred to the National Park Service, which had been created the previous year.

visitor_centers.title: Albright Visitor Center
visitor_centers.description: Here you can gather park information, orient yourself to the rest of the park (including road conditions, and campground and lodge availability), shop for souvenirs, explore exhibits to learn more about the wildlife and history of Yellowstone, join a ranger program, pick up a fishing permit (fees are payable by debit or credit card), or use the restrooms.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Front of the Albright Visitor Center

visitor_centers.title: Canyon Visitor Education Center
visitor_centers.description: Stop by the Canyon Visitor Education Center to learn more about the geologic story of the area, including the Yellowstone volcano, and view a room-size relief map of Yellowstone.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Visit the Canyon Visitor Education Center to learn about geology of the Yellowstone volcano and get park orientation information.

visitor_centers.title: Fishing Bridge Visitor Center and Trailside Museum
visitor_centers.description: The distinctive stone-and-log architecture of this old building—known as "parkitecture"—was one of several prototypes for park buildings around the country.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Visitors walk toward the Fishing Bridge Visitor Center and Trailside Museum to get park information or learn about the ecology of Yellowstone Lake.

visitor_centers.title: Grant Visitor Center
visitor_centers.description: Fire plays an important, natural role in the ecosystems found in Yellowstone National Park and the surrounding region.
visitor_centers.image_caption: At the Grant Visitor Center you can learn how forest fires play an important part in keeping Yellowstone healthy.

visitor_centers.title: Madison Information Station and Trailside Museum
visitor_centers.description: Located about halfway between Old Faithful and West Yellowstone, you can stop here for park information as visitors have been doing since 1930.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Visitors can get park information at the Madison Information Station or simply enjoy the view from the back patio.

campgrounds.title: Bridge Bay Campground
campgrounds.description: Bridge Bay Campground—elevation 7,800 feet (2377 m)—is located near Yellowstone Lake, one of the largest, high-elevation, fresh-water lakes in North America.
campgrounds.image_caption: Bridge Bay Campground is located close to the Bridge Bay Marina and Yellowstone Lake.

campgrounds.title: Canyon Campground
campgrounds.description: Canyon Campground—elevation 7,900 feet (2408 m)—lies in a lodgepole pine forest at Canyon Village, south of the Washburn range and near the breath-taking Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone River.
campgrounds.image_caption: Campsite in the Canyon Campground

campgrounds.title: Fishing Bridge RV Park
campgrounds.description: Fishing Bridge RV Park—elevation 7,800 feet (2377 m)—is located near the Yellowstone River where it exits Yellowstone Lake on its way toward the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone.
campgrounds.image_caption: Fishing Bridge General Store

campgrounds.title: Grant Village Campground
campgrounds.description: Grant Campground—elevation 7,800 feet (2377 m)—is located in Grant Village, just off the Grand Loop Road at the south end of Yellowstone Lake.
campgrounds.image_caption: Grant Village Campground

campgrounds.title: Indian Creek Campground
campgrounds.description: Located about eight miles south of Mammoth Hot Springs on the road to Norris, Indian Creek Campground—elevation 7,300 feet (2225 m)—sits near the base of the Gallatin Mountains and offers breathtaking views of Electric Peak.
campgrounds.image_caption: Indian Creek Campground

source_pack.operating_hours:
Yellowstone is open daily, year-round, although activities and services are limited at night and certain times of year. The park has five entrance stations, but not all entrance stations are open year-round. Make sure to carefully read about access at each station at different times of year. And remember, all dates are weather-dependent.

Please note that camping is possible only in designated campgrounds.

profile.hook: Yosemite National Park ( yoh-SEM-ih-tee) is a national park of the United States in California.
profile.summary: Yosemite National Park ( yoh-SEM-ih-tee) is a national park of the United States in California. It is bordered on the southeast by the Sierra National Forest and on the northwest by Stanislaus National Forest. The park is managed by the National Park Service and covers 1,187 mi2 (3,070 km2) in four counties – centered in Tuolumne and Mariposa, extending north and east to Mono and south to Madera.
profile.story: Here is the story of Yosemite National Park. Yosemite National Park ( yoh-SEM-ih-tee) is a national park of the United States in California. It is bordered on the southeast by the Sierra National Forest and on the northwest by Stanislaus National Forest. The park is managed by the National Park Service and covers 1,187 mi2 (3,070 km2) in four counties – centered in Tuolumne and Mariposa, extending north and east to Mono and south to Madera. Designated a World Heritage Site in 1984, Yosemite is internationally recognized for its granite cliffs, waterfalls, clear streams, groves of giant sequoia, lakes, mountains, meadows, glaciers, and biological diversity. Almost 95 percent of the park is designated wilderness. Yosemite is one of the largest and least fragmented habitat blocks in the Sierra Nevada mountain range. Its geology is characterized by granite and remnants of older rock. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.wiki_extract:
Yosemite National Park ( yoh-SEM-ih-tee) is a national park of the United States in California. It is bordered on the southeast by the Sierra National Forest and on the northwest by Stanislaus National Forest. The park is managed by the National Park Service and covers 1,187 mi2 (3,070 km2) in four counties – centered in Tuolumne and Mariposa, extending north and east to Mono and south to Madera. Designated a World Heritage Site in 1984, Yosemite is internationally recognized for its granite cliffs, waterfalls, clear streams, groves of giant sequoia, lakes, mountains, meadows, glaciers, and biological diversity. Almost 95 percent of the park is designated wilderness. Yosemite is one of the largest and least fragmented habitat blocks in the Sierra Nevada mountain range.
Its geology is characterized by granite and remnants of older rock. About 10 million years ago, the Sierra Nevada was uplifted and tilted to form its unique slopes, which increased the steepness of stream and river beds, forming deep, narrow canyons. About one million years ago, glaciers formed at higher elevations. They moved downslope, cutting and sculpting the U-shaped Yosemite Valley.

things_to_do.title: Scenic Driving in Yosemite
things_to_do.description: All of the roads in Yosemite National Park are scenic, but the most famous scenic drive is along the Tioga Road, a 46-mile (62 km) drive from Crane Flat to Tioga Pass.
things_to_do.image_caption: Scenic Driving in Yosemite: Yosemite Valley

things_to_do.title: Rock Climbing in Yosemite
things_to_do.description: Yosemite is one of the world's greatest climbing areas.
things_to_do.image_caption: Climber on ledge on rock face in Yosemite Valley

things_to_do.title: Ride a Bike in Yosemite Valley
things_to_do.description: Cycling is a great way to experience the park and allows you to cover more ground during your time here.
things_to_do.image_caption: Bicyclists on bike paths in Yosemite

things_to_do.title: Hiking in Yosemite
things_to_do.description: From a short walk to the base of a waterfall to a multi-day hike deep into the Yosemite Wilderness, taking a hike is one of the best ways to experience Yosemite's spectacular scenery.

source_pack.extract: Not just a great valley, but a shrine to human foresight, the strength of granite, the power of glaciers, the persistence of life, and the tranquility of the High Sierra. First protected in 1864, Yosemite National Park is best known for its waterfalls, but within its nearly 1,200 square miles, you can find deep valleys, grand meadows, ancient giant sequoias, a vast wilderness area, and much more.

profile.hook: Arches National Park is a national park of the United States in eastern Utah.
profile.summary: Arches National Park is a national park of the United States in eastern Utah. The park is adjacent to the Colorado River, 4 mi (6 km) north of Moab, Utah. The park contains more than 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the well-known Delicate Arch, which constitute the highest density of natural arches in the world.
profile.story: Here is the story of Arches National Park. Arches National Park is a national park of the United States in eastern Utah. The park is adjacent to the Colorado River, 4 mi (6 km) north of Moab, Utah. The park contains more than 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the well-known Delicate Arch, which constitute the highest density of natural arches in the world. It also contains a variety of other unique geological resources and formations. The national park lies above an underground evaporite layer or salt bed, which is the main cause of the formation of the arches, spires, balanced rocks, sandstone fins, and eroded monoliths in the area. The park consists of 310.31 km2 (76,680 acres; 119.81 sq mi; 31,031 ha) of high desert located on the Colorado Plateau. The highest elevation in the park is 5,653 ft (1,723 m) at Elephant Butte, and the lowest elevation is 4,085 ft (1,245 m) at the visitor center. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.wiki_extract:
Arches National Park is a national park of the United States in eastern Utah. The park is adjacent to the Colorado River, 4 mi (6 km) north of Moab, Utah. The park contains more than 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the well-known Delicate Arch, which constitute the highest density of natural arches in the world. It also contains a variety of other unique geological resources and formations. The national park lies above an underground evaporite layer or salt bed, which is the main cause of the formation of the arches, spires, balanced rocks, sandstone fins, and eroded monoliths in the area.
The park consists of 310.31 km2 (76,680 acres; 119.81 sq mi; 31,031 ha) of high desert located on the Colorado Plateau. The highest elevation in the park is 5,653 ft (1,723 m) at Elephant Butte, and the lowest elevation is 4,085 ft (1,245 m) at the visitor center. The park receives an average of less than 10 in (250 mm) of rain annually.
Administered by the National Park Service, the area was originally designated a national monument on April 12, 1929 before being redesignated a national park on November 12, 1971. The park received more than 1.8 million visitors in 2021.

summary.title: Zion National Park

profile.hook: Zion National Park is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah near the town of Springdale.
profile.summary: Zion National Park is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah near the town of Springdale. Located at the junction of the Colorado Plateau, Great Basin, and Mojave Desert regions, the park has a unique geography and a variety of life zones that allow for unusual plant and animal diversity. Numerous plant species as well as 289 species of birds, 75 mammals (including 19 species of bat), and 32 reptiles inhabit the park's four life zones: desert, riparian, woodland, and coniferous forest.
profile.story: Here is the story of Zion National Park. Zion National Park is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah near the town of Springdale. Located at the junction of the Colorado Plateau, Great Basin, and Mojave Desert regions, the park has a unique geography and a variety of life zones that allow for unusual plant and animal diversity. Numerous plant species as well as 289 species of birds, 75 mammals (including 19 species of bat), and 32 reptiles inhabit the park's four life zones: desert, riparian, woodland, and coniferous forest. Zion National Park includes mountains, canyons, buttes, mesas, monoliths, rivers, slot canyons, and natural arches. The lowest point in the park is 3,666 ft (1,117 m) at Coalpits Wash and the highest peak is 8,726 ft (2,660 m) at Horse Ranch Mountain. A prominent feature of the 229-square-mile (590 km2) park is Zion Canyon, which is 15 miles (24 km) long and up to 2,640 ft (800 m) deep. The canyon walls are reddish and tan-colored Navajo Sandstone eroded by the North Fork of the Virgin River. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.wiki_extract:
Zion National Park is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah near the town of Springdale. Located at the junction of the Colorado Plateau, Great Basin, and Mojave Desert regions, the park has a unique geography and a variety of life zones that allow for unusual plant and animal diversity. Numerous plant species as well as 289 species of birds, 75 mammals (including 19 species of bat), and 32 reptiles inhabit the park's four life zones: desert, riparian, woodland, and coniferous forest. Zion National Park includes mountains, canyons, buttes, mesas, monoliths, rivers, slot canyons, and natural arches. The lowest point in the park is 3,666 ft (1,117 m) at Coalpits Wash and the highest peak is 8,726 ft (2,660 m) at Horse Ranch Mountain. A prominent feature of the 229-square-mile (590 km2) park is Zion Canyon, which is 15 miles (24 km) long and up to 2,640 ft (800 m) deep. The canyon walls are reddish and tan-colored Navajo Sandstone eroded by the North Fork of the Virgin River. The park attracted 4,946,592 visitors in 2024.
Human habitation of the area started about 8,000 years ago with small family groups of Native Americans, one of which was the semi-nomadic Basketmaker Ancestral Puebloans (who used to be called Anasazi by early non-indigenous archeologists) (c. 300 CE).

summary.title: Bryce Canyon National Park
summary.hook: Bryce Canyon National Park () is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah.
summary.short_description: Bryce Canyon National Park () is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah.

profile.hook: Bryce Canyon National Park () is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah.
profile.summary: Bryce Canyon National Park () is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah. The major feature of the park is Bryce Canyon, which, despite its name, is not a canyon but a collection of giant natural amphitheaters along the eastern side of the Paunsaugunt Plateau. Bryce is distinctive due to geological structures called hoodoos, formed by frost weathering and stream erosion of the river and lake bed sedimentary rock.
profile.story: Here is the story of Bryce Canyon National Park. Bryce Canyon National Park () is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah. The major feature of the park is Bryce Canyon, which, despite its name, is not a canyon but a collection of giant natural amphitheaters along the eastern side of the Paunsaugunt Plateau. Bryce is distinctive due to geological structures called hoodoos, formed by frost weathering and stream erosion of the river and lake bed sedimentary rock. The red, orange, and white colors of the rocks provide distinctive views for park visitors. Bryce Canyon National Park is much smaller and sits at a much higher elevation than nearby Zion National Park. The rim at Bryce varies from 8,000 to 9,000 feet (2,400 to 2,700 m). The area is in portions of Garfield County and Kane County. places.wiki_extract:
Bryce Canyon National Park () is a national park of the United States located in southwestern Utah. The major feature of the park is Bryce Canyon, which, despite its name, is not a canyon but a collection of giant natural amphitheaters along the eastern side of the Paunsaugunt Plateau. Bryce is distinctive due to geological structures called hoodoos, formed by frost weathering and stream erosion of the river and lake bed sedimentary rock. The red, orange, and white colors of the rocks provide distinctive views for park visitors. Bryce Canyon National Park is much smaller and sits at a much higher elevation than nearby Zion National Park. The rim at Bryce varies from 8,000 to 9,000 feet (2,400 to 2,700 m).
The area is in portions of Garfield County and Kane County. It was settled by Mormon pioneers in the 1850s and was named after Ebenezer Bryce, who homesteaded in the area in 1874. The area was originally designated as a national monument by President Warren G. Harding in 1923 and was redesignated as a national park by Congress in 1928. The park covers 35,835 acres (55.992 sq mi; 14,502 ha; 145.02 km2) and receives substantially fewer visitors than Zion National Park (nearly 4.3 million in 2016) or Grand Canyon National Park (almost 6 million in 2016), largely due to Bryce's more remote location.

photos.caption: Viewpoints of the iconic Bryce Amphitheater are located along the first 3 miles of the park road and are a popular destination at sunrise.
Here at 8,000' (2438 m) winter comes early and stays late. Snow transforms the landscape and requires seasonal closures in some areas and trails.
Bryce Canyon's clean air and dark night skies are some of its most precious and vulnerable resources. Full moon nights also provide a unique experience of seeing the park after dark.
More strenuous trails below the rim provide up-close views of the hoodoo rock spires, while easier walks along the rim give perspective from above.
The park's highest elevations at its southern end and backcountry areas provide views of over 100 miles (161 km) atop high steep cliffs.

things_to_do.title: Birdwatching
things_to_do.description: 175 different species of birds have been documented to frequent Bryce Canyon National Park.
things_to_do.image_caption: Steller's Jays are one of the park's most common year-round bird species.

things_to_do.title: Bristlecone Loop Trail
things_to_do.description: The Bristlecone Loop, an easy 1 mile hike, meanders through the forest atop this highest portion of the park, reaching elevations over 9,100 feet (2778 m).
things_to_do.image_caption: Views from the Bristlecone Loop Trail

things_to_do.title: Swamp Canyon Loop
things_to_do.description: Swamp Canyon appears relatively small and sheltered from the overlook, allowing the viewer to develop a more intimate connection with the landscape than some of the grander viewpoints in the park.
things_to_do.image_caption: The Swamp Canyon Loop takes you into the forested backcountry wilderness area of the park.

things_to_do.title: Plan a Picnic
things_to_do.description: Looking for a good place to enjoy a picnic?
things_to_do.image_caption: Rainbow Point, at the end of the Southern Scenic Drive, is a great place to stop for a picnic.

things_to_do.title: Figure-8 Combination
things_to_do.description: The Figure-8 Combination combines three amphitheater hikes, Queen's Garden, Navajo Loop, and the Peekaboo Loop, into one ultimate hike!
things_to_do.image_caption: Views along the connector trail between the Queen's/Navajo trails and the Peekaboo Loop.

things_to_do.title: Queen's/Navajo Combination Loop
things_to_do.description: Our most popular hike in the park and the one we most recommend to first-time visitors.
things_to_do.image_caption: Hikers approach an archway on the Queen's Garden side of the Combination Loop

things_to_do.title: Queen's Garden Trail
things_to_do.description: The Queen's Garden Trail is the least difficult of the trails descending from the rim into the Bryce Canyon Amphitheater.
things_to_do.image_caption: The view of the Queen's Garden Trail from the Rim.

things_to_do.title: Bryce Canyon Evening Program
things_to_do.description: Evening programs, typically offered from Memorial Day through Labor Day, offer topics that go beyond the rim!
things_to_do.image_caption: Bryce Canyon Evening Programs now take place in the Lodge Auditorium.

things_to_see.title: A Forest Reborn wayside
things_to_see.description: On June 14, 2009, a lightning strike started the Bridge Fire that burned 3,947 acres in Bryce Canyon National Park and Dixie National Forest.
things_to_see.image_caption: After the fire, nutrients were recycled back into the soil, and a variety of species returned.

things_to_see.title: A Lasting Legacy wayside
things_to_see.description: In 1933, during the Great Depression, President Franklin D.
things_to_see.image_caption: Today’s conservation programs and groups continue the legacy of the CCC.

things_to_see.title: Additional Parking Lot
things_to_see.description: This additional parking lot is located just southeast of the Visitor Center and provides all-day and oversize vehicle parking.
things_to_see.image_caption: Parking

things_to_see.title: Agua Canyon
things_to_see.description: At Agua Canyon two prominent hoodoos command attention.
things_to_see.image_caption: Agua Canyon provides views of the Hunter and the Rabbit--two iconic hoodoos in the park

things_to_see.title: Agua Canyon Connecting Trail Trailhead at Ponderosa Canyon
things_to_see.description: Trail Closed due to Rockslide This trail descends 1.6 miles (4.0 km) to its junction with the Under the Rim Trail approximately 1,000 feet (305 m) below.
things_to_see.image_caption: The Agua Canyon Connecting Trail leads down from Ponderosa Point

things_to_see.title: Agua Canyon Trail wayside
things_to_see.description: Here, the Agua Canyon Connecting Trail descends to the rugged 22.9-mile (36.9 km) Under-the-Rim backcountry trail, which travels north to Bryce Point and south to Rainbow Point.
things_to_see.image_caption: The park backcountry offers rugged terrain for adventurous hikers seeking a less-traveled trail.

things_to_see.title: Air Quality wayside
things_to_see.description: Bryce Canyon is known for its stunning views and vistas.
things_to_see.image_caption: On a clear day from this point you can see Navajo Mountain, 80 miles (129 km) away.

things_to_see.title: An Ever-changing Landscape wayside
things_to_see.description: Colorful Layers Pure limestone is white, but here, iron deposits have oxidized, or rusted, producing the yellows, oranges, reds, and browns.
things_to_see.image_caption: Bryce Canyon is ever-changing.

visitor_centers.title: Bryce Canyon Visitor Center
visitor_centers.description: The Bryce Canyon Visitor Center might be one of your first stops when visiting Bryce Canyon National Park.
visitor_centers.image_caption: The park Visitor Center is located 1 mile past the park entrance and is open most days at 8 a.m.

campgrounds.title: North Campground
campgrounds.description: Located across the road from the Visitor Center and is comprised of 100 sites in 4 loops; A, B, C, D.
campgrounds.image_caption: Both tents and RVs are welcome in North Campground

campgrounds.title: Sunset Campground
campgrounds.description: Located west of Sunset Point, approximately 1.5 miles south of the Bryce Canyon Visitor Center, and is comprised of 99 sites in 3 loops; Loop A is primarily for RVs; Loops B & C are tent-only.
campgrounds.image_caption: Both RVs and Tent campers are welcome in Sunset Campground

source_pack.operating_hours: Bryce Canyon National Park is open 365 days a year, 24 hours a day.

source_pack.extract: Hoodoos (irregular columns of rock) exist on every continent, but here is the largest concentration found anywhere on Earth. Situated along a high plateau at the top of the Grand Staircase, the park's high elevations include numerous life communities, fantastic dark skies, and geological wonders that defy description.

profile.summary: Monument Valley is a region of the Colorado Plateau characterized by a cluster of sandstone buttes, with the largest reaching 1,000 ft (300 m) above the valley floor. The most famous butte formations are located in northeastern Arizona along the Utah–Arizona state line. The valley is considered sacred by the Navajo Nation, the Native American people within whose reservation it lies.
profile.story: Here is the story of Monument Valley. Monument Valley is a region of the Colorado Plateau characterized by a cluster of sandstone buttes, with the largest reaching 1,000 ft (300 m) above the valley floor. The most famous butte formations are located in northeastern Arizona along the Utah–Arizona state line. The valley is considered sacred by the Navajo Nation, the Native American people within whose reservation it lies. Monument Valley has been featured in many forms of media since the 1930s. Famed director John Ford used the location for a number of his Westerns. Film critic Keith Phipps wrote that "its five square miles [13 km2] have defined what decades of moviegoers think of when they imagine the American West". For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

profile.story: Here is the story of Canyonlands National Park. Canyonlands National Park is a national park of the United States located in southeastern Utah near the town of Moab. The park preserves a colorful landscape eroded into numerous canyons, mesas, and buttes by the Colorado River, the Green River, and their respective tributaries. Legislation creating the park was signed into law by President Lyndon B. Johnson on September 12, 1964. The park is divided into four districts: the Island in the Sky, the Needles, the Maze, and the combined rivers—the Green and Colorado—which carved two large canyons into the Colorado Plateau. While these areas share a primitive desert atmosphere, each retains its own character. Author Edward Abbey, a frequent visitor, described the Canyonlands as "the most weird, wonderful, magical place on earth—there is nothing else like it anywhere". For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

photos.caption: Mesa Arch, at Island in the Sky, is a great spot for photographers.
Pothole Point Trail
The Maze is the most remote district of the park. Visiting requires four-wheel drive, self-reliance, and extra time.
The White Rim Road at Island in the Sky is a popular road for mountain bikers.
The Needles, pinnacles of Cedar Mesa Sandstone, are visible in many parts of the Needles District, including this view in Chesler Park.
Boating the Colorado and Green rivers is a popular activity at Canyonlands (permit required).

things_to_do.title: Hiking at Canyonlands
things_to_do.description: Canyonlands has hundreds of miles of hiking trails which explore the park’s natural and cultural features.
things_to_do.image_caption: Careful planning helps you have a safe, enjoyable hike.

things_to_do.title: Boating in Canyonlands
things_to_do.description: The Colorado and Green rivers have played a significant role in shaping the landscape of Canyonlands.
things_to_do.image_caption: Kayaking sections of flatwater is a unique way to experience the park from the bottom up.

things_to_do.title: Stargazing Events in Southeast Utah
things_to_do.description: Rangers occasionally lead stargazing programs and events at parks throughout southeast Utah.

things_to_do.title: Canyonlands Junior Ranger Program
things_to_do.description: There are a few ways to become a junior ranger at Canyonlands.
things_to_do.image_caption: Earning your own badge is a a fun way to learn about the park.

things_to_do.title: Rock Climbing at Canyonlands
things_to_do.description: The sandstone towers at the Island in the Sky attract the most rock climbers.
things_to_do.image_caption: Rock climbing at Canyonlands is limited due to poor rock quality and lack of routes.

things_to_do.title: Backcountry Camping in Canyonlands
things_to_do.description: What better way to connect with this wilderness of countless canyons than on foot?
things_to_do.image_caption: Tents underneath the stars in Canyonlands

things_to_do.title: Experience the Shafer Trail
things_to_do.description: The Shafer Trail is a challenging, unpaved backcountry road for recreational users seeking the experience of a lifetime.
things_to_do.image_caption: The Shafer Trail winds 1,500 feet (457 m) down colorful sandstone walls.

things_to_do.title: Auto Touring in Canyonlands
things_to_do.description: There are hundreds of miles of unpaved roads in Canyonlands, providing access to various campsites, trailheads, and viewpoints in the park's backcountry.
things_to_do.image_caption: A jeep traverses a sandy road at the bottom of a red rock canyon.

things_to_see.title: Alcove Spring Trail
things_to_see.description: After descending 1,400 ft (4276 m) past a large alcove, the trail meanders in a wide canyon to the base of the notable Moses and Zeus towers.
things_to_see.image_caption: An impressive alcove along a streaked sandstone wall.

things_to_see.title: Aztec Butte Trail
things_to_see.description: This trail climbs two sandstone buttes to outstanding views and stone granaries built by Indigenous people centuries ago.
things_to_see.image_caption: Aztec Butte Granary

things_to_see.title: Aztec Butte: The Value of Storage
things_to_see.description: Aztec Butte: The Value of Storage
things_to_see.image_caption: A sign along the Aztec Butte Trail provides information on human history.

things_to_see.title: Big Spring Canyon Overlook and Trailhead
things_to_see.description: Big Spring rests at the end of the Needles Park Road.
things_to_see.image_caption: The view from Big Spring Overlook

things_to_see.title: Big Spring Canyon Overlook: Protecting Wilderness
things_to_see.description: Big Spring Canyon Overlook: Protecting Wilderness
things_to_see.image_caption: A sign at Big Spring Canyon Overlook provides information on the wilderness.

things_to_see.title: Big Spring Canyon to Elephant Canyon Loop Trail
things_to_see.description: This loop features extended hiking on slickrock benches and mesa tops overlooking canyons.
things_to_see.image_caption: Elephant Canyon

things_to_see.title: Big Spring Canyon to Wooden Shoe Canyon Loop Trail
things_to_see.description: A great introduction to the landscape of The Needles, this route connects two canyons for a loop across varied terrain.
things_to_see.image_caption: Big Spring Canyon Trail

things_to_see.title: Buck Canyon Overlook
things_to_see.description: From a paved overlook at 6,240 feet (1,902 m) in elevation, enjoy sweeping vistas to the west and across the Colorado River canyon.
things_to_see.image_caption: Buck Canyon Overlook

visitor_centers.description: The remote Hans Flat (Maze) Ranger Station is normally open daily year-round.

profile.summary: Mesa Verde National Park is a national park of the United States and UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Montezuma County, Colorado, and the only World Heritage Site in Colorado. The park protects some of the best-preserved Ancestral Puebloan ancestral sites in the United States. Established by Congress and President Theodore Roosevelt in 1906, the park occupies 52,485 acres (212 km2) near the Four Corners region of the American Southwest.
profile.story: Here is the story of Mesa Verde National Park. Mesa Verde National Park is a national park of the United States and UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Montezuma County, Colorado, and the only World Heritage Site in Colorado. The park protects some of the best-preserved Ancestral Puebloan ancestral sites in the United States. Established by Congress and President Theodore Roosevelt in 1906, the park occupies 52,485 acres (212 km2) near the Four Corners region of the American Southwest. With more than 5,000 sites, including 600 cliff dwellings, it is the largest archaeological preserve in the United States. Mesa Verde (Spanish for "green table", or more specifically "green table mountain") is best known for structures such as Cliff Palace, one of the largest cliff dwellings in North America. Starting c. 7500 BC Mesa Verde was seasonally inhabited by a group of nomadic Paleo-Indians known as the Foothills Mountain Complex. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.audio_script: Here is the story of Mesa Verde National Park. Mesa Verde National Park is a national park of the United States and UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Montezuma County, Colorado, and the only World Heritage Site in Colorado. The park protects some of the best-preserved Ancestral Puebloan ancestral sites in the United States. Established by Congress and President Theodore Roosevelt in 1906, the park occupies 52,485 acres (212 km2) near the Four Corners region of the American Southwest. With more than 5,000 sites, including 600 cliff dwellings, it is the largest archaeological preserve in the United States. Mesa Verde (Spanish for "green table", or more specifically "green table mountain") is best known for structures such as Cliff Palace, one of the largest cliff dwellings in North America. Starting c. 7500 BC Mesa Verde was seasonally inhabited by a group of nomadic Paleo-Indians known as the Foothills Mountain Complex. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.
places.wiki_extract:
Mesa Verde National Park is a national park of the United States and UNESCO World Heritage Site located in Montezuma County, Colorado, and the only World Heritage Site in Colorado. The park protects some of the best-preserved Ancestral Puebloan ancestral sites in the United States.
Established by Congress and President Theodore Roosevelt in 1906, the park occupies 52,485 acres (212 km2) near the Four Corners region of the American Southwest. With more than 5,000 sites, including 600 cliff dwellings, it is the largest archaeological preserve in the United States. Mesa Verde (Spanish for "green table", or more specifically "green table mountain") is best known for structures such as Cliff Palace, one of the largest cliff dwellings in North America.
Starting c. 7500 BC Mesa Verde was seasonally inhabited by a group of nomadic Paleo-Indians known as the Foothills Mountain Complex. The variety of projectile points found in the region indicates they were influenced by surrounding areas, including the Great Basin, the San Juan Basin, and the Rio Grande Valley. Later, Archaic people established semi-permanent rock shelters in and around the mesa. By 1000 BC, the Basketmaker culture emerged from the local Archaic population, and by 750 AD the Ancestral Puebloans had developed from the Basketmaker culture.
The Pueblo people survived using a combination of hunting, gathering, and subsistence farming of crops such as corn, beans, and squash (the "Three Sisters").

things_to_do.title: Visit Mesa Verde's Historic Administrative District
things_to_do.description: Take a walk amid the early park structures that comprise the Historic Administrative District of Mesa Verde National Park.
things_to_do.image_caption: The Chapin Mesa Archeological Museum is part of the historic district.

things_to_do.title: Explore the Mesa Top Loop Road
things_to_do.description: The 6-mile (10 km) Mesa Top Loop takes you through 700 years of Ancestral Pueblo history.

things_to_see.title: Balcony House Parking Area
things_to_see.description: The Balcony House Parking Area is where ranger-guided tours of Balcony House begin.
things_to_see.image_caption: View from the Balcony House Parking Area

things_to_see.title: Cliff Canyon Overlook
things_to_see.description: Cliff Canyon Overlook affords a vast view of Cliff Canyon.
things_to_see.image_caption: View from Cliff Canyon Overlook

things_to_see.title: Cliff Palace Loop Road
things_to_see.description: Cliff Palace Loop Road is a 6-mile (10-km) drive with overlooks of various cliff dwellings and access to ranger-guided tours at Cliff Palace and Balcony House.
things_to_see.image_caption: View from the Cliff Palace Overlook

things_to_see.title: Cliff Palace Overlook
things_to_see.description: Cliff Palace Overlook is a magnificent view point for Cliff Palace, the largest cliff dwelling in the park.
things_to_see.image_caption: View from Cliff Palace Overlook

things_to_see.title: Cliff Palace View
things_to_see.description: Cliff Palace View
things_to_see.image_caption: Visitors view Cliff Palace from across canyon.

things_to_see.title: Far View Community Sites
things_to_see.description: The level, unpaved ¾-mile trail (1.2 km) leads you through a mesa top community includes Far View House, four other villages, and a dry reservoir.
things_to_see.image_caption: Looking across Pipe Shrine House at Far View House

things_to_see.title: Far View Lodge
things_to_see.description: Far View Lodge, the only lodging inside of Mesa Verde National Park, sits on a high shoulder offering panoramic vistas and wildlife watching.
things_to_see.image_caption: Kiva Room at Far View Lodge

things_to_see.title: Farming Terrace Trail
things_to_see.description: Beginning and ending on the spur road to Cedar Tree Tower, this 0.5-mile (0.8 km) loop trail leads through a series of check dams built by the Ancestral Pueblo people over 800 years ago.
things_to_see.image_caption: Ancient check dams built along a steep slope created small farming terraces.

visitor_centers.title: Mesa Verde Museum
visitor_centers.description: Located at milepost 20, the Mesa Verde Mesa Museum offers exhibits and cultural objects that provide insight into the Ancestral Pueblo lifeways.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Entrance to Chapin Mesa Archeological Museum, an Historic Landmark and part of the Historic Administration District.

visitor_centers.title: Mesa Verde Visitor and Research Center
visitor_centers.description: Located at the park entrance, off Highway 160, this is the park’s primary facility for orienting visitors to opportunities within the park and surrounding area.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Mesa Verde Visitor and Research Center's Welcome Plaza

campgrounds.title: Morefield Campground
campgrounds.description: Morefield Campground is four miles from the park entrance.
campgrounds.image_caption: Morefield's campsites are situated on loop roads that extend through a high grassy canyon filled with Gambel Oak scrub, native flowers, deer, and wild turkeys.

source_pack.operating_hours:
The park is open daily except for emergency conditions. The Visitor & Research Center and Mesa Verde Museum are open all year but closed Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Years Day. Ancestral sites, picnic areas, and trails are open 8 am to sunset. Some park areas including Wetherill Mesa, campground, and other visitor services are closed during winter.

Cliff dwelling tours are mid-May through late October. Reservations are required and available 14 days in advance of tour date online at www.recreation.gov.

source_pack.source_note: Official NPS source pack with live park details captured at catalog build time.
source_pack.extract: For over 700 years, the Ancestral Pueblo people built thriving communities on the mesas and in the cliffs of Mesa Verde. Today, the park protects the rich cultural heritage of 27 Pueblos and Tribes and offers visitors a spectacular window into the past. This World Heritage Site and International Dark Sky Park is home to over a thousand species, including several that live nowhere else on earth.

summary.title: Bears Ears National Monument

profile.hook: Bears Ears National Monument is a United States national monument located in San Juan County in southeastern Utah, established by President Barack Obama by presidential proclamation on December 28, 2016.
profile.summary: Bears Ears National Monument is a United States national monument located in San Juan County in southeastern Utah, established by President Barack Obama by presidential proclamation on December 28, 2016. The monument protects 1,351,849 acres (2,112.264 sq mi; 5,470.74 km2) of public land surrounding the Bears Ears—a pair of buttes—and the Indian Creek corridor rock climbing area. The Native American names for the buttes have the same meaning in each of the languages represented in the region.
profile.story: Here is the story of Bears Ears National Monument. Bears Ears National Monument is a United States national monument located in San Juan County in southeastern Utah, established by President Barack Obama by presidential proclamation on December 28, 2016. The monument protects 1,351,849 acres (2,112.264 sq mi; 5,470.74 km2) of public land surrounding the Bears Ears—a pair of buttes—and the Indian Creek corridor rock climbing area. The Native American names for the buttes have the same meaning in each of the languages represented in the region. The names are listed in the presidential proclamation as "Hoon’Naqvut, Shash Jáa [sic], Kwiyaghatʉ Nükavachi/Kwiyagatu Nukavachi, Ansh An Lashokdiwe"—all four mean "Bears Ears". The area within the monument is largely undeveloped and contains a wide array of historic, cultural and natural resources. The monument is co-managed by the Bureau of Land Management and United States Forest Service (through the Manti-La Sal National Forest), along with a coalition of five local Native American tribes: the Navajo Nation, Hopi, Ute Mountain Ute, Ute Indian Tribe of the Uintah and Ouray Reservation, and the Pueblo of Zuni, all of which have ancestral ties to the region. The monument includes the area around the Bears Ears formation and adjacent land to the southeast along the Comb Ridge formation, as well as Indian Creek Canyon to the northeast. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

The Native American names for the buttes have the same meaning in each of the languages represented in the region. The names are listed in the presidential proclamation as "Hoon’Naqvut, Shash Jáa [sic], Kwiyaghatʉ Nükavachi/Kwiyagatu Nukavachi, Ansh An Lashokdiwe"—all four mean "Bears Ears". places.wiki_extract:
Bears Ears National Monument is a United States national monument located in San Juan County in southeastern Utah, established by President Barack Obama by presidential proclamation on December 28, 2016. The monument protects 1,351,849 acres (2,112.264 sq mi; 5,470.74 km2) of public land surrounding the Bears Ears—a pair of buttes—and the Indian Creek corridor rock climbing area. The Native American names for the buttes have the same meaning in each of the languages represented in the region. The names are listed in the presidential proclamation as "Hoon’Naqvut, Shash Jáa [sic], Kwiyaghatʉ Nükavachi/Kwiyagatu Nukavachi, Ansh An Lashokdiwe"—all four mean "Bears Ears".
The area within the monument is largely undeveloped and contains a wide array of historic, cultural and natural resources. The monument is co-managed by the Bureau of Land Management and United States Forest Service (through the Manti-La Sal National Forest), along with a coalition of five local Native American tribes: the Navajo Nation, Hopi, Ute Mountain Ute, Ute Indian Tribe of the Uintah and Ouray Reservation, and the Pueblo of Zuni, all of which have ancestral ties to the region.
The monument includes the area around the Bears Ears formation and adjacent land to the southeast along the Comb Ridge formation, as well as Indian Creek Canyon to the northeast. The monument also includes the Valley of the Gods to the south, the western part of the Manti-La Sal National Forest's Monticello unit, and the Dark Canyon Wilderness to the north and west.
A proclamation issued by President Donald Trump on December 4, 2017, reduced the monument by 85% to 201,876 acres (315 sq mi; 817 km2)—an unprecedented and exceptionally large reduction in the history of U.S. national monuments.  President Joe Biden restored the territory removed by Trump on October 8, 2021.

profile.summary: Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument is a U.S. national monument and UNESCO biosphere reserve located in southern Arizona that shares a border with the Mexican state of Sonora. The park is one of the few places in the United States where the senita and organ pipe cactus grow wild.
profile.story: Here is the story of Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument. Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument is a U.S. national monument and UNESCO biosphere reserve located in southern Arizona that shares a border with the Mexican state of Sonora. The park is one of the few places in the United States where the senita and organ pipe cactus grow wild. Along with these species, many other types of cacti and other flora native to the Yuma Desert section of the Sonoran Desert region grow in the park. Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument is 517 sq mi (1,340 km2) in size. In 1976 the monument was declared a Biosphere Reserve by UNESCO, and in 1977 95% of the monument was declared a wilderness area. Land for the monument was donated by the Arizona state legislature to the federal government during Prohibition, knowing that the north–south road would be improved and make contraband alcohol easier to import from Mexico. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.wiki_extract:
Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument is a U.S. national monument and UNESCO biosphere reserve located in southern Arizona that shares a border with the Mexican state of Sonora. The park is one of the few places in the United States where the senita and organ pipe cactus grow wild. Along with these species, many other types of cacti and other flora native to the Yuma Desert section of the Sonoran Desert region grow in the park. Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument is 517 sq mi (1,340 km2) in size. In 1976 the monument was declared a Biosphere Reserve by UNESCO, and in 1977 95% of the monument was declared a wilderness area.
Land for the monument was donated by the Arizona state legislature to the federal government during Prohibition, knowing that the north–south road would be improved and make contraband alcohol easier to import from Mexico. In 1937 the land was officially opened as a national monument.
At the north entrance of the park is the unincorporated community of Why, Arizona; the town of Lukeville, Arizona at the park's southern border is a border crossing point to Sonoyta, Sonora, Mexico.
Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument is bordered to the northwest by Cabeza Prieta National Wildlife Refuge and to the east by the Tohono Oʼodham Indian Reservation.

things_to_do.title: Hike the Historic Old County Road!
things_to_do.description: Enjoy scenic views along an 8-mile one-way hike as you explore a historic wagon road through the Sonoran Desert!
things_to_do.image_caption: Enjoy the quiet wilderness as you hike a historic wagon road.

things_to_do.title: Hike the Senita Basin Loop
things_to_do.description: Easy loop trail located in a low elevation area on the south side of the Puerto Blanco Mountains.
things_to_do.image_caption: Some of the best examples of senita cacti are located along the road leading into Senita Basin.

things_to_do.title: Hike the Estes Canyon/Bull Pasture Loop
things_to_do.description: The moderate Estes Canyon trail provides ample wildlife and wildflower viewing opportunities while a steep, strenuous climb up the Bull Pasture spur trail will reward visitors with spectacular views of the Sonoran Desert.
things_to_do.image_caption: The 800 foot climb to Bull Pasture rewards visitors with some of the best views in the monument.

things_to_do.title: Hike to Lost Cabin
things_to_do.description: Looking for a lengthier hike that showcases Organ Pipe’s mining history?
things_to_do.image_caption: Lost Cabin, like most of the structures in the park, was constructed using stone found locally.

things_to_do.title: Hike to Victoria Mine
things_to_do.description: Experience Organ Pipe’s mining history by hiking to Victoria Mine, one of the oldest prospecting sites in southwest Arizona and the heart of gold and silver mining in the Organ Pipe area.
things_to_do.image_caption: Miners could find food and other essentials at the Victoria Mine store.

things_to_do.title: Unearth Dripping Springs Mine
things_to_do.description: Stumble upon an old copper mine while exploring along the trail in the Puerto Blanco Mountains!
things_to_do.image_caption: Enjoy beautiful mountain scenery as you wind your way to Dripping Springs Mine.

things_to_do.title: Hike Alamo Canyon
things_to_do.description: The Alamo Canyon Trail follows an old road to a historic ranch house and corral, and provides ample wildlife and wildflower viewing opportunities.
things_to_do.image_caption: The Alamo Canyon trail leads to a historic ranch once run by Birdie and Bill Miller.

things_to_do.title: Hike Arch Canyon
things_to_do.description: One of the most photographed sites along the Ajo Mountain Drive are the double arches carved into the mountains.
things_to_do.image_caption: Moonrise over the double arches

campgrounds.title: Alamo Canyon Campground
campgrounds.description: Alamo Canyon Campground is a secluded, four-site campground nestled against rocky mountains at the mouth of Alamo Canyon.
campgrounds.image_caption: The surrounding landscape at the campground is full of life.

campgrounds.title: Twin Peaks Campground
campgrounds.description: Twin Peaks Campground is the main, developed campground at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, located 1.5 miles from the Kris Eggle Visitor Center and Highway 85.
campgrounds.image_caption: Campers enjoy a relaxing afternoon at the Twin Peaks Campground.

source_pack.operating_hours:
The monument is open 365 days a year and park roads and trails are open 24 hours. There is no gate at the monument entrance; entrance fees are paid for at the visitor center. There is no dispersed camping allowed anywhere in the monument. US-Mexico Border hours are 6 am-8 pm.

The main season for visitors is December through the end of April, during the mild winter months and wildflower season. Campgrounds may fill during this time; check Recreation.gov for campsite availability.

source_pack.extract: Look closely. Look again. The sights and sounds of Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, an International Biosphere Reserve, reveal a thriving community of plants and animals. Human stories echo throughout the Sonoran Desert, chronicling thousands of years of desert living. A wilderness hike, a scenic drive, or a night of camping will expose you to a living desert abounding with hidden life.

profile.hook: Chaco Culture National Historical Park is a United States National Historical Park in the American Southwest hosting a large concentration of pre-Columbian indigenous ruins of pueblos.
profile.summary: Chaco Culture National Historical Park is a United States National Historical Park in the American Southwest hosting a large concentration of pre-Columbian indigenous ruins of pueblos. The park is in northwestern New Mexico, between Albuquerque and Farmington, in a remote canyon cut by the Chaco Wash. Containing the most sweeping collection of ancient ruins north of Mexico, the park preserves one of the most important cultural and historical areas in the United States.
profile.story: Here is the story of Chaco Culture National Historical Park. Chaco Culture National Historical Park is a United States National Historical Park in the American Southwest hosting a large concentration of pre-Columbian indigenous ruins of pueblos. The park is in northwestern New Mexico, between Albuquerque and Farmington, in a remote canyon cut by the Chaco Wash. Containing the most sweeping collection of ancient ruins north of Mexico, the park preserves one of the most important cultural and historical areas in the United States. Between 900 and 1150, Chaco Canyon was a major cultural center for the Ancestral Puebloans. Chacoans quarried sandstone blocks and hauled timber from great distances, assembling 15 major complexes that remained the largest buildings ever built in North America until the 19th century. Evidence of archaeoastronomy at Chaco has been proposed, with the "Sun Dagger" petroglyph at Fajada Butte a popular example. Many Chacoan buildings may have been aligned to capture the solar and lunar cycles, requiring generations of astronomical observations and centuries of skillfully coordinated construction. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.audio_script: Here is the story of Chaco Culture National Historical Park. Chaco Culture National Historical Park is a United States National Historical Park in the American Southwest hosting a large concentration of pre-Columbian indigenous ruins of pueblos. The park is in northwestern New Mexico, between Albuquerque and Farmington, in a remote canyon cut by the Chaco Wash. Containing the most sweeping collection of ancient ruins north of Mexico, the park preserves one of the most important cultural and historical areas in the United States. Between 900 and 1150, Chaco Canyon was a major cultural center for the Ancestral Puebloans. Chacoans quarried sandstone blocks and hauled timber from great distances, assembling 15 major complexes that remained the largest buildings ever built in North America until the 19th century. Evidence of archaeoastronomy at Chaco has been proposed, with the "Sun Dagger" petroglyph at Fajada Butte a popular example. Many Chacoan buildings may have been aligned to capture the solar and lunar cycles, requiring generations of astronomical observations and centuries of skillfully coordinated construction. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.
places.wiki_extract:
Chaco Culture National Historical Park is a United States National Historical Park in the American Southwest hosting a large concentration of pre-Columbian indigenous ruins of pueblos. The park is in northwestern New Mexico, between Albuquerque and Farmington, in a remote canyon cut by the Chaco Wash. Containing the most sweeping collection of ancient ruins north of Mexico, the park preserves one of the most important cultural and historical areas in the United States.
Between 900 and 1150, Chaco Canyon was a major cultural center for the Ancestral Puebloans. Chacoans quarried sandstone blocks and hauled timber from great distances, assembling 15 major complexes that remained the largest buildings ever built in North America until the 19th century. Evidence of archaeoastronomy at Chaco has been proposed, with the "Sun Dagger" petroglyph at Fajada Butte a popular example. Many Chacoan buildings may have been aligned to capture the solar and lunar cycles, requiring generations of astronomical observations and centuries of skillfully coordinated construction. Climate change is thought to have led to the emigration of Chacoans and the eventual abandonment of the canyon, beginning with a 50-year drought that began in 1130.
The Chacoan cultural sites, in the arid, sparsely populated Four Corners region, are fragile. Concerns about erosion caused by tourists have led to the closure of Fajada Butte to the public.

photos.caption: Chaco Culture National Historical Park is an International Dark Sky Park.
Welcome to Chaco Culture National Historical Park!
Most walls seen at Chaco Culture NHP are close to 1,000 years old.
Observe many ravens while visiting Chaco Culture NHP.
Pueblo Bonito is the largest great house within Chaco Canyon.
Chaco Culture National Historical Park is home to impressive pottery built by the ancestral Pueblo people.

things_to_see.title: Casa Rinconada
things_to_see.description: Located 6 miles from the visitor center on the 9-mile Canyon Loop Drive.
things_to_see.image_caption: Next time you find yourself in Chaco Canyon, be sure to visit the Casa Rinconada community.

things_to_see.title: Chetro Ketl
things_to_see.description: Located 4 1/2 miles from the Visitor Center on the 9-mile Canyon Loop Drive.
things_to_see.image_caption: The walk through Chetro Ketl offers numerous examples of the remodeling, updating, or repurposing..

things_to_see.title: Fajada Butte
things_to_see.description: Fajada Butte is a unique part of the landscape of Chaco Canyon and can be seen from miles away.
things_to_see.image_caption: Visitors can observe Fajada Butte from an overlook accessed from the park road.

things_to_see.title: Hungo Pavi
things_to_see.description: Located 2 miles from the visitor on the 9-mile loop drive.
things_to_see.image_caption: Travel in small groups to lessen our impacts to this fragile site.

things_to_see.title: Pueblo Bonito
things_to_see.description: Located 4 1/2 miles from the Visitor Center on the 9-mile Canyon Loop Drive.
things_to_see.image_caption: Pueblo Bonito is the only great house visitors are allowed to walk through the rooms of.

things_to_see.title: Pueblo del Arroyo
things_to_see.description: Located 5 1/2 miles from the Visitor Center on the 9-mile Canyon Loop Drive.
things_to_see.image_caption: An unusual feature is the tri-wall kiva. Only ten tri-wall kivas have been found in the Southwest.

things_to_see.title: Una Vida
things_to_see.description: Una Vida is one of Chaco's earliest great houses built around 850 CE (Common Era).
things_to_see.image_caption: Built during the same period as Bonito, this impressive structure sits well above the canyon floor.

visitor_centers.title: Chaco Culture National Historical Park Visitor Center
visitor_centers.description: This is Chaco Culture's only visitor center.
visitor_centers.image_caption: The Visitor Center is open 9:00am-5:00pm.

campgrounds.title: Gallo Campground
campgrounds.description: Gallo Campground, located one mile (1.6 km) east of the visitor center, is open year-round.
campgrounds.image_caption: There are 24 available sites in the Gallo Campground and 2 group sites.

source_pack.operating_hours: All hiking trails and archaeological sites are open from 7am until 9:00pm from May 1-October 31. The visitor center is open from 9:00am-5:00pm daily. At 8:30 PM, you must be leaving or if you are camping, you need to be back in the campground.

source_pack.extract: Explore the monumental structures and breathtaking landscape at Chaco, a thriving regional center for the ancestral Pueblo people from 850 to 1250 CE (Common Era), through hiking & biking trails, ranger guided tours & programs, and more. Chaco Canyon is a sacred and deeply personal place for many Indigenous peoples throughout the Southwest. Please visit with respect.

photos.caption: Sunsets are one of the most popular times to visit White Sands. Visitors can experience sunset every day of the year.
The dunes at White Sands cover 275 square miles of the Tularosa Basin.
The wind shapes and moves the dunes as a whole but gives each individual dune the texture of ripples.
Fall is a wonderful time to visit as most of our plants with leaves change beautiful colors.
As the sun changes its angle through the sky it allows shadow to roll across the dunes.

source_pack.operating_hours: The park gates always open at 7:00 am with closing hours changing with sunset. The park closes 30 minutes after local sunset. Local sunset is 8 minutes earlier than Alamogordo's predicted sunset. Sunset times can be found at https://sunrise-sunset.org/us/alamogordo-nm

source_pack.extract: Rising from the heart of the Tularosa Basin is one of the world's great natural wonders - the glistening white sands of New Mexico. Great wave-like dunes of gypsum sand have engulfed 275 square miles of desert, creating the world's largest gypsum dunefield. White Sands National Park preserves a major portion of this unique dunefield, along with the plants and animals that live here.

photos.caption: The view at the park entrance contrasts giant dunes and snow-capped mountains.
Rent specially designed sand sleds to slide on the dry sand.  Note: cardboard, snow sleds, saucers, and other items don't slide on dry sand.
Elk are sometimes seen by visitors along the park entrance road or County Lane 6, primarily fall through spring.
Spectacular alpine lakes are part of the backcountry of Great Sand Dunes National Preserve
Sand Creek Basin, part of Great Sand Dunes National Preserve, contains lush forests and alpine lakes.
Fall is a pretty time to visit the park, with great colors and generally nice weather.

things_to_do.title: Explore the Dunes
things_to_do.description: Explore the tallest dunes in North America!
things_to_do.image_caption: Explore any part of the dunefield!

things_to_do.title: Sandboarding and Sand Sledding
things_to_do.description: Sandboarding and sand sledding are the most popular activities on the dunes.
things_to_do.image_caption: Two girls speed down a dune on a specially designed sand sled.

things_to_do.title: Splash in Medano Creek
things_to_do.description: Medano Creek is a popular seasonal stream enjoyed by all ages.
things_to_do.image_caption: People of all ages enjoy splashing, floating, and playing in Medano Creek each spring.

things_to_do.title: Hike Montville Nature Trail or Mosca Pass Trail
things_to_do.description: Just above the dunefield are montane woodlands of pinon and ponderosa pine, subalpine aspen and conifer forests, alpine lakes and rugged mountains over 13,000 feet (4,000 m) in elevation.
things_to_do.image_caption: Montville Trail and Mosca Pass Trail feature aspen and conifer forests along a small creek.

things_to_do.title: Experience the Night
things_to_do.description: Half the park is after dark!
things_to_do.image_caption: The Milky Way is visible on moonless nights in summer and fall.

things_to_do.title: Hike Medano Lake and Mount Herard
things_to_do.description: Take your 4WD vehicle around the dunes and up to Medano Pass, hike to a small alpine lake, then continue to the summit of 13,297 ft (4,053 m) Mount Herard for an aerial perspective on the entire dunefield!
things_to_do.image_caption: The incredible 360 degree views from the summit of Mount Herard include the dunefield and more.

things_to_do.title: Hike Sand Creek Lakes
things_to_do.description: Sand Creek Lakes are part of the alpine watershed of Great Sand Dunes National Preserve.
things_to_do.image_caption: Sand Creek Lakes are a completely different environment from the dunes!

things_to_do.title: 4WD Medano Pass Primitive Road
things_to_do.description: Drive a a 4WD around the eastern edge of the dunes then over a forested mountain pass.
things_to_do.image_caption: A Jeep crosses a creek in fall on the Medano Pass Primitive Road

things_to_see.title: Hidden Dune
things_to_see.description: Hidden Dune is one of the two tallest dunes in North America, located in a remote part of the dunefield of Great Sand Dunes.
things_to_see.image_caption: Hidden Dune is at right; the false summit that hides it is at left.

things_to_see.title: High Dune on First Ridge
things_to_see.description: The most popular to climb of the tall dunes at Great Sand Dunes
things_to_see.image_caption: High Dune on First Ridge is illuminated by the rising sun.

things_to_see.title: Medano Creek
things_to_see.description: Medano Creek is a popular seasonal stream enjoyed by all ages.
things_to_see.image_caption: During peak flow, Medano Creek flows down in waves, a rare phenomenon called 'surge flow'.

things_to_see.title: Medano Pass
things_to_see.description: Medano Pass was likely the historic route used by Zebulon Pike in January 1807 as he led an expedition to explore this region.
things_to_see.image_caption: Fall is one of the prettiest times to drive the Medano Pass Road, as aspens turn gold.

things_to_see.title: Mosca Pass
things_to_see.description: Just above the dunefield are woodlands of pinon and ponderosa pine, and subalpine aspen and conifer forests.
things_to_see.image_caption: Fall is one of the prettiest times to hike Mosca Pass Trail, as aspens turn gold.

things_to_see.title: Sand Creek Basin
things_to_see.description: Sand Creek Basin is part of the alpine watershed of Great Sand Dunes National Preserve.
things_to_see.image_caption: View from Marble Mountain into Sand Creek Basin; Upper Sand Creek Lake is visible at left.

things_to_see.title: Star Dune
things_to_see.description: One of the two tallest dunes in North America, pyramid-shaped Star Dune rises 741 feet (225 m).
things_to_see.image_caption: Star Dune is one of the two tallest dunes in North America.

things_to_see.title: The Dunefield
things_to_see.description: The centerpiece of Great Sand Dunes National Park and Preserve is the 30-square-mile (78-square-km) dunefield, featuring the tallest dunes in North America.
things_to_see.image_caption: The 30-square-mile dunefield is the centerpiece of Great Sand Dunes National Park and Preserve

visitor_centers.title: Entrance Station
visitor_centers.description: Purchase park passes at the Entrance Station, located on the main entrance road.
visitor_centers.image_caption: A ranger greets a visitor at the entrance station with a park brochure

visitor_centers.title: Great Sand Dunes Visitor Center
visitor_centers.description: The visitor center offers restrooms, a water fill station, interactive exhibits, and a 20-minute video about the park.
visitor_centers.image_caption: Great Sand Dunes Visitor Center is a adobe-styled stucco building.

campgrounds.title: Backpacking Campsites in the Dunes or Forest
campgrounds.description: The most popular and unique backpacking option is camping in the 30-square-mile dunefield, beyond the major dune ridgeline (about 1.5 mile hike minimum over dunes).
campgrounds.image_caption: Backpacking in the dunes is a unique experience, with open views of the sky.

campgrounds.title: Medano Pass Primitive Road Campsites
campgrounds.description: Roadside camping is permitted at 21 campsites in Great Sand Dunes National Preserve beginning 5.2 miles from where the road begins near Piñon Flats Campground.
campgrounds.image_caption: A full-size SUV, Jeep or truck with 4WD is required to access Medano Pass Primitive Road campsites

campgrounds.title: Piñon Flats Campground
campgrounds.description: Piñon Flats is a National Park Service campground located one mile north of the Visitor Center, open April through October.
campgrounds.image_caption: Pinon Flats Campground

source_pack.operating_hours: The park and preserve are open 24/7 all year

source_pack.extract: The tallest dunes in North America are the centerpiece in a diverse landscape of grasslands, wetlands, forests, alpine lakes, and tundra. Stay on a moonless night to experience countless stars in this International Dark Sky Park! Open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week year-round. There are no timed entries or reservations to visit.

summary.hook: Badlands National Park (Lakota: Makȟóšiča) is a national park of the United States in southwestern South Dakota.

profile.hook: Badlands National Park (Lakota: Makȟóšiča) is a national park of the United States in southwestern South Dakota.
profile.summary: Badlands National Park (Lakota: Makȟóšiča) is a national park of the United States in southwestern South Dakota. The park protects 242,756 acres (379.3 sq mi; 982.4 km2) of sharply eroded buttes and pinnacles, along with the largest undisturbed mixed grass prairie in the United States. The National Park Service manages the park, with the South Unit being co-managed with the Oglala Lakota tribe.
profile.story: Here is the story of Badlands National Park. Badlands National Park (Lakota: Makȟóšiča) is a national park of the United States in southwestern South Dakota. The park protects 242,756 acres (379.3 sq mi; 982.4 km2) of sharply eroded buttes and pinnacles, along with the largest undisturbed mixed grass prairie in the United States. The National Park Service manages the park, with the South Unit being co-managed with the Oglala Lakota tribe. Located within the White River drainage, the Badlands Wilderness protects 64,144 acres (100.2 sq mi; 259.6 km2) of the park's North Unit as a designated wilderness area, and is one site where the black-footed ferret, one of the most endangered mammals in the world, was reintroduced to the wild. The South Unit, or Stronghold District, includes sites of 1890s Ghost Dances, a former United States Air Force bomb and gunnery range, and Red Shirt Table, the park's highest point at 3,340 feet (1,020 m). Authorized as Badlands National Monument on March 4, 1929, it was not established until January 25, 1939. Badlands was redesignated a national park on November 10, 1978. For a Trailhead stop, treat it as more than a photo marker: give yourself time to understand the place, check current access, and look around before moving on.

places.wiki_extract:
Badlands National Park (Lakota: Makȟóšiča) is a national park of the United States in southwestern South Dakota. The park protects 242,756 acres (379.3 sq mi; 982.4 km2) of sharply eroded buttes and pinnacles, along with the largest undisturbed mixed grass prairie in the United States. The National Park Service manages the park, with the South Unit being co-managed with the Oglala Lakota tribe.
Located within the White River drainage, the Badlands Wilderness protects 64,144 acres (100.2 sq mi; 259.6 km2) of the park's North Unit as a designated wilderness area, and is one site where the black-footed ferret, one of the most endangered mammals in the world, was reintroduced to the wild. The South Unit, or Stronghold District, includes sites of 1890s Ghost Dances, a former United States Air Force bomb and gunnery range, and Red Shirt Table, the park's highest point at 3,340 feet (1,020 m).
Authorized as Badlands National Monument on March 4, 1929, it was not established until January 25, 1939. Badlands was redesignated a national park on November 10, 1978. Under the Mission 66 plan, the Ben Reifel Visitor Center was constructed for the monument in 1957–58. The park also administers the nearby Minuteman Missile National Historic Site. The movies Dances with Wolves (1990) and Thunderheart (1992) were partially filmed in Badlands National Park.

photos.caption: Summer storms are frequently violent and unpredictable.
The yellow mounds are peaking out of the formations in this photo.
Badlands formations are very rugged and often have sharp peaks.
The park has seasonal changes in colors so plan ahead!
Winter in Badlands National Park often creates snow covered wonderlands throughout the park.

source_pack.extract: The rugged beauty of the Badlands draws visitors from around the world. These striking geologic deposits contain one of the world’s richest fossil beds. Ancient horses and rhinos once roamed here. The park’s 244,000 acres protect an expanse of mixed-grass prairie where bison, bighorn sheep, prairie dogs, and black-footed ferrets live today.

profile.story: Here is the story of Devils Tower. Devils Tower (also known as Matȟó Thípila or Bear Lodge) is a laccolithic butte, composed of igneous rock in the Bear Lodge Ranger District of the Black Hills, near Hulett and Sundance in Crook County, northeastern Wyoming, above the Belle Fourche River. It rises 1,267 feet (386 m) above the Belle Fourche River, standing 867 ft (264 m) from summit to base. The summit is 5,112 ft (1,558 m) above sea level. Devils Tower National Monument was the first United States national monument, established on September 24, 1906, by President Theodore Roosevelt. The monument's boundary encloses an area of 1,347 acres (545 ha). source_pack.source_note: Official NPS source pack with live park details captured at catalog build time.
source_pack.extract: The Tower is an astounding geologic feature that protrudes out of the prairie surrounding the Black Hills. It is considered sacred by Northern Plains Indians and indigenous people. Hundreds of parallel cracks make it one of the finest crack climbing areas in North America. Devils Tower entices us to learn more, explore more and define our place in the natural and cultural world.

summary.title: Mount Rushmore
summary.hook: The Mount Rushmore National Memorial is a national memorial centered on a colossal sculpture carved into the granite face of Mount Rushmore (Lakota: Tȟuŋkášila Šákpe, or Six Grandfathers) in the Black Hills near Keystone, South Dakota, United States.
